question:
1. Prove that, for every positive integer \( n \),
\[
\sqrt{1}+\sqrt{2}+\cdots+\sqrt{n}
\]
is more than \( \frac{2}{3} n \sqrt{n} \) and less than
\[
\frac{4 n+3}{6} \sqrt{n} .
\]
solution:
Solution. For \( k \) a positive integer and \( k-1 \leq x<k \), we have \( \sqrt{k}>\sqrt{x} \). Therefore \( \sqrt{k}>\int_{k-1}^{k} \sqrt{x} d x \). Adding, we get
\[
\sqrt{1}+\sqrt{2}+\cdots+\sqrt{n}>\int_{0}^{n} \sqrt{x} d x=\frac{2}{3} n^{3 / 2}
\]

Since the graph of \( \sqrt{x} \) is concave downward, it is clear from the diagram that
\[
\frac{1}{2}(\sqrt{k-1}+\sqrt{k})<\int_{k-1}^{k} \sqrt{x} d x
\]
(i.e., the trapezoidal approximation to the integral is too small). Hence
\[
\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{1}+\sqrt{2}+\cdots+\sqrt{n}= & \frac{1}{2}(\sqrt{0}+\sqrt{1})+\frac{1}{2}(\sqrt{1}+\sqrt{2}) \\
& +\cdots+\frac{1}{2}(\sqrt{n}-1+\sqrt{n})+\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{n} \\
< & \int_{0}^{n} \sqrt{x} d x+\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{n}=\frac{4 n+3}{6} \sqrt{n} .
\end{aligned}
\]

The inequality (1) can be proved analytically, of course. We need the standard theorem for estimating the error in the trapezoidal rule for approximating an integral. It is:

Let \( f \) be continuous on the closed interval \( [a, b] \) and assume \( f^{\prime \prime} \) exists on the open interval \( (a, b) \). Then there exists a point \( \eta \) in \( (a, b) \) such that
\[
\int_{a}^{b} f(x) d x=\frac{1}{2}(b-a)[f(a)+f(b)]-\frac{1}{12} f^{\prime \prime}(\eta)(b-a)^{3} .
\]

For the case at hand, take \( f(x)=\sqrt{x}, a=k-1, b=k \). Since \( f^{\prime \prime} \) is negative on ( \( 0, \infty \) ), inequality (1) follows.

descriptive_long.question:
1. Prove that, for every positive integer \( countsize \),
\[
\sqrt{1}+\sqrt{2}+\cdots+\sqrt{countsize}
\]
is more than \( \frac{2}{3} countsize \sqrt{countsize} \) and less than
\[
\frac{4 countsize+3}{6} \sqrt{countsize} .
\]
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. For \( iterindex \) a positive integer and \( iterindex-1 \leq varvalue<iterindex \), we have \( \sqrt{iterindex}>\sqrt{varvalue} \). Therefore \( \sqrt{iterindex}>\int_{iterindex-1}^{iterindex} \sqrt{varvalue} d varvalue \). Adding, we get
\[
\sqrt{1}+\sqrt{2}+\cdots+\sqrt{countsize}>\int_{0}^{countsize} \sqrt{varvalue} d varvalue=\frac{2}{3} countsize^{3 / 2}
\]

Since the graph of \( \sqrt{varvalue} \) is concave downward, it is clear from the diagram that
\[
\frac{1}{2}(\sqrt{iterindex-1}+\sqrt{iterindex})<\int_{iterindex-1}^{iterindex} \sqrt{varvalue} d varvalue
\]
(i.e., the trapezoidal approximation to the integral is too small). Hence
\[
\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{1}+\sqrt{2}+\cdots+\sqrt{countsize}= & \frac{1}{2}(\sqrt{0}+\sqrt{1})+\frac{1}{2}(\sqrt{1}+\sqrt{2}) \\
& +\cdots+\frac{1}{2}(\sqrt{countsize-1}+\sqrt{countsize})+\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{countsize} \\
< & \int_{0}^{countsize} \sqrt{varvalue} d varvalue+\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{countsize}=\frac{4 countsize+3}{6} \sqrt{countsize} .
\end{aligned}
\]

The inequality (1) can be proved analytically, of course. We need the standard theorem for estimating the error in the trapezoidal rule for approximating an integral. It is:

Let \( function \) be continuous on the closed interval \( [lowerlimit, upperlimit] \) and assume \( function^{\prime \prime} \) exists on the open interval \( (lowerlimit, upperlimit) \). Then there exists a point \( etapoint \) in \( (lowerlimit, upperlimit) \) such that
\[
\int_{lowerlimit}^{upperlimit} function(varvalue) d varvalue=\frac{1}{2}(upperlimit-lowerlimit)[function(lowerlimit)+function(upperlimit)]-\frac{1}{12} function^{\prime \prime}(etapoint)(upperlimit-lowerlimit)^{3} .
\]

For the case at hand, take \( function(varvalue)=\sqrt{varvalue}, lowerlimit=iterindex-1, upperlimit=iterindex \). Since \( function^{\prime \prime} \) is negative on ( \( 0, \infty \) ), inequality (1) follows.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
1. Prove that, for every positive integer \( hurricane \),
\[
\sqrt{1}+\sqrt{2}+\cdots+\sqrt{hurricane}
\]
is more than \( \frac{2}{3} hurricane \sqrt{hurricane} \) and less than
\[
\frac{4 hurricane+3}{6} \sqrt{hurricane} .
\]
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. For \( pineapple \) a positive integer and \( pineapple-1 \leq constellation<pineapple \), we have \( \sqrt{pineapple}>\sqrt{constellation} \). Therefore \( \sqrt{pineapple}>\int_{pineapple-1}^{pineapple} \sqrt{constellation} d\,constellation \). Adding, we get
\[
\sqrt{1}+\sqrt{2}+\cdots+\sqrt{hurricane}>\int_{0}^{hurricane} \sqrt{constellation} d\,constellation=\frac{2}{3} hurricane^{3 / 2}
\]

Since the graph of \( \sqrt{constellation} \) is concave downward, it is clear from the diagram that
\[
\frac{1}{2}(\sqrt{pineapple-1}+\sqrt{pineapple})<\int_{pineapple-1}^{pineapple} \sqrt{constellation} d\,constellation
\]
(i.e., the trapezoidal approximation to the integral is too small). Hence
\[
\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{1}+\sqrt{2}+\cdots+\sqrt{hurricane}= & \frac{1}{2}(\sqrt{0}+\sqrt{1})+\frac{1}{2}(\sqrt{1}+\sqrt{2}) \\
& +\cdots+\frac{1}{2}(\sqrt{hurricane}-1+\sqrt{hurricane})+\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{hurricane} \\
< & \int_{0}^{hurricane} \sqrt{constellation} d\,constellation+\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{hurricane}=\frac{4 hurricane+3}{6} \sqrt{hurricane} .
\end{aligned}
\]

The inequality (1) can be proved analytically, of course. We need the standard theorem for estimating the error in the trapezoidal rule for approximating an integral. It is:

Let \( marigold \) be continuous on the closed interval \( [blueberry, evergreen] \) and assume \( marigold^{\prime \prime} \) exists on the open interval \( (blueberry, evergreen) \). Then there exists a point \( compassrose \) in \( (blueberry, evergreen) \) such that
\[
\int_{blueberry}^{evergreen} marigold(constellation) d\,constellation=\frac{1}{2}(evergreen-blueberry)[marigold(blueberry)+marigold(evergreen)]-\frac{1}{12} marigold^{\prime \prime}(compassrose)(evergreen-blueberry)^{3} .
\]

For the case at hand, take \( marigold(constellation)=\sqrt{constellation}, blueberry=pineapple-1, evergreen=pineapple \). Since \( marigold^{\prime \prime} \) is negative on ( \( 0, \infty \) ), inequality (1) follows.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Problem:
<<<
1. Prove that, for every positive integer \( tinycount \),
\[
\sqrt{1}+\sqrt{2}+\cdots+\sqrt{tinycount}
\]
is more than \( \frac{2}{3} tinycount \sqrt{tinycount} \) and less than
\[
\frac{4 tinycount+3}{6} \sqrt{tinycount} .
\]
>>>
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution:
<<<
Solution. For \( continuousvalue \) a positive integer and \( continuousvalue-1 \leq discretesequence<continuousvalue \), we have \( \sqrt{continuousvalue}>\sqrt{discretesequence} \). Therefore \( \sqrt{continuousvalue}>\int_{continuousvalue-1}^{continuousvalue} \sqrt{discretesequence} d discretesequence \). Adding, we get
\[
\sqrt{1}+\sqrt{2}+\cdots+\sqrt{tinycount}>\int_{0}^{tinycount} \sqrt{discretesequence} d discretesequence=\frac{2}{3} tinycount^{3 / 2}
\]

Since the graph of \( \sqrt{discretesequence} \) is concave downward, it is clear from the diagram that
\[
\frac{1}{2}(\sqrt{continuousvalue-1}+\sqrt{continuousvalue})<\int_{continuousvalue-1}^{continuousvalue} \sqrt{discretesequence} d discretesequence
\]
(i.e., the trapezoidal approximation to the integral is too small). Hence
\[
\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{1}+\sqrt{2}+\cdots+\sqrt{tinycount}= & \frac{1}{2}(\sqrt{0}+\sqrt{1})+\frac{1}{2}(\sqrt{1}+\sqrt{2}) \\
& +\cdots+\frac{1}{2}(\sqrt{tinycount}-1+\sqrt{tinycount})+\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{tinycount} \\
< & \int_{0}^{tinycount} \sqrt{discretesequence} d discretesequence+\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{tinycount}=\frac{4 tinycount+3}{6} \sqrt{tinycount} .
\end{aligned}
\]

The inequality (1) can be proved analytically, of course. We need the standard theorem for estimating the error in the trapezoidal rule for approximating an integral. It is:

Let \( constantvalue \) be continuous on the closed interval \( [upperbound, lowerbound] \) and assume \( constantvalue^{\prime \prime} \) exists on the open interval \( (upperbound, lowerbound) \). Then there exists a point \( boundarypoint \) in \( (upperbound, lowerbound) \) such that
\[
\int_{upperbound}^{lowerbound} constantvalue(discretesequence) d discretesequence=\frac{1}{2}(lowerbound-upperbound)[constantvalue(upperbound)+constantvalue(lowerbound)]-\frac{1}{12} constantvalue^{\prime \prime}(boundarypoint)(lowerbound-upperbound)^{3} .
\]

For the case at hand, take \( constantvalue(discretesequence)=\sqrt{discretesequence}, upperbound=continuousvalue-1, lowerbound=continuousvalue \). Since \( constantvalue^{\prime \prime} \) is negative on ( \( 0, \infty \) ), inequality (1) follows.
>>>

garbled_string.question:
Prove that, for every positive integer \( ghsodnpe \),
\[
\sqrt{1}+\sqrt{2}+\cdots+\sqrt{ghsodnpe}
\]
is more than \( \frac{2}{3} ghsodnpe \sqrt{ghsodnpe} \) and less than
\[
\frac{4 ghsodnpe+3}{6} \sqrt{ghsodnpe} .
\]
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. For \( cibshdfe \) a positive integer and \( cibshdfe-1 \leq qbnvtyio<cibshdfe \), we have \( \sqrt{cibshdfe}>\sqrt{qbnvtyio} \). Therefore \( \sqrt{cibshdfe}>\int_{cibshdfe-1}^{cibshdfe} \sqrt{qbnvtyio} d qbnvtyio \). Adding, we get
\[
\sqrt{1}+\sqrt{2}+\cdots+\sqrt{ghsodnpe}>\int_{0}^{ghsodnpe} \sqrt{qbnvtyio} d qbnvtyio=\frac{2}{3} ghsodnpe^{3 / 2}
\]

Since the graph of \( \sqrt{qbnvtyio} \) is concave downward, it is clear from the diagram that
\[
\frac{1}{2}(\sqrt{cibshdfe-1}+\sqrt{cibshdfe})<\int_{cibshdfe-1}^{cibshdfe} \sqrt{qbnvtyio} d qbnvtyio
\]
(i.e., the trapezoidal approximation to the integral is too small). Hence
\[
\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{1}+\sqrt{2}+\cdots+\sqrt{ghsodnpe}= & \frac{1}{2}(\sqrt{0}+\sqrt{1})+\frac{1}{2}(\sqrt{1}+\sqrt{2}) \\
& +\cdots+\frac{1}{2}(\sqrt{ghsodnpe}-1+\sqrt{ghsodnpe})+\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{ghsodnpe} \\
< & \int_{0}^{ghsodnpe} \sqrt{qbnvtyio} d qbnvtyio+\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{ghsodnpe}=\frac{4 ghsodnpe+3}{6} \sqrt{ghsodnpe} .
\end{aligned}
\]

The inequality (1) can be proved analytically, of course. We need the standard theorem for estimating the error in the trapezoidal rule for approximating an integral. It is:

Let \( zlkmvrea \) be continuous on the closed interval \( [wypoqmdk, ltraxcve] \) and assume \( zlkmvrea^{\prime\prime} \) exists on the open interval \( (wypoqmdk, ltraxcve) \). Then there exists a point \( snvkrpqa \) in \( (wypoqmdk, ltraxcve) \) such that
\[
\int_{wypoqmdk}^{ltraxcve} zlkmvrea(qbnvtyio) d qbnvtyio=\frac{1}{2}(ltraxcve-wypoqmdk)[zlkmvrea(wypoqmdk)+zlkmvrea(ltraxcve)]-\frac{1}{12} zlkmvrea^{\prime\prime}(snvkrpqa)(ltraxcve-wypoqmdk)^{3} .
\]

For the case at hand, take \( zlkmvrea(qbnvtyio)=\sqrt{qbnvtyio}, wypoqmdk=cibshdfe-1, ltraxcve=cibshdfe \). Since \( zlkmvrea^{\prime\prime} \) is negative on ( \( 0, \infty \) ), inequality (1) follows.

kernel_variant.question:
For the partial sums  
  S_n := 1^{1/3}+2^{1/3}+\cdots +n^{1/3}, n \in  \mathbb{N},  

(a) prove the refined two-sided estimate  
  (3/4)n^{4/3}+\frac{1}{2} n^{1/3} < S_n < (3/4)(n+1)^{4/3} - \frac{3}{4} + \frac{1}{2} (n+1)^{1/3}.  

(b) show that the coefficient 3/4 is best possible, i.e.  
  lim _{n\to \infty } S_n / n^{4/3} = 3/4,  
so an inequality of the form S_n \geq  c n^{4/3} eventually fails when c > 3/4, and  
S_n \leq  c (n+1)^{4/3} fails for c < 3/4.
kernel_variant.solution:
(\approx  185 words)  

Step 1 Preparations.  
Let f(x)=x^{1/3}.  Then f'(x)=\frac{1}{3}x^{-2/3}>0, f''(x)=-2/9 x^{-5/3}<0, so f is increasing and concave on [0,\infty ).

Step 2 Lower bound (trapezoidal rule from the left).  
Because f is concave, for k \geq  1  
  \int _{k-1}^{k}f(x)dx < \frac{1}{2}[f(k-1)+f(k)].  
Summing k=1\ldots n and rearranging,  
  S_n > \int _{0}^{n}f(x)dx + \frac{1}{2} f(n)  
   = (3/4)n^{4/3}+\frac{1}{2} n^{1/3}.  

Step 3 Upper bound (trapezoidal rule from the right).  
Again by concavity,  
  \frac{1}{2}[f(k)+f(k+1)] < \int _{k}^{k+1}f(x)dx.  
Adding k=1\ldots n and discarding the positive term \frac{1}{2} f(n+1),  
  S_n < \int _{1}^{n+1}f(x)dx - \frac{1}{2} f(1)  
   = (3/4)(n+1)^{4/3} - \frac{3}{4} + \frac{1}{2} (n+1)^{1/3}.  

Step 4 Optimality of 3/4.  
Divide the definition of S_n by n^{4/3}.  By monotonicity,  
  \int _{0}^{n}f(x)dx < S_n < \int _{1}^{n+1}f(x)dx,  
whence  
  3/4 < S_n / n^{4/3} < 3/4\cdot [(n+1)/n]^{4/3}.  
Let n\to \infty ; the squeeze theorem yields lim S_n/n^{4/3}=3/4.  
Therefore any c > 3/4 eventually violates S_n \geq  c n^{4/3}, and any c < 3/4 eventually violates S_n \leq  c (n+1)^{4/3}.

Thus parts (a) and (b) are proved.

_replacement_note.reason: Original kernel variant was too easy compared to the original problem
